Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Defendant Cross Complainants Supplemental Briefing Motion Re Appropriate Remedies To Be Granted In Response To Defendants January 19 Motion
Off Calendar Apr-13-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. JOSE MANUEL A. DE CASTRO OF THE DE CASTRO LAW GROUP PC, TEL. NO. 1-310-270-9877 EXT. 09817 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF BY TELEPHONE APPEARANCE;
ATTY. NATALIE PIERCE OF THE LITTLER LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 1-415-288-6321 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANTS.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Demeter Defendants' motion re appropriate remedies to be granted in response to their January 19 motion is denied as to their request for an order compelling production of documents on plaintiff Chase Brogan's third privilege log and granted in part as to monetary sanctions. Mr. Brogan is required to pay $17,600 in sanctions to defendants. Mr. Brogan satisfied his burden of showing that all documents that defendants seek to be produced by this motion (the documents listed on the third privilege log reflecting communications by Mr. Brogan with his father, Ben Van de Bunt and Stephanie Blackman) are privileged. "The privilege attaches to otherwise protected confidential communications so long as the client reasonably believed that the person he or she consulted was authorized to practice law ' even though the supposed 'lawyer' has never been admitted to practice." (Wagner, Fairbank and Epstein, California Practice Guide: Civil Trials and Evidence (The Rutter Group 2016) 8:1995). Mr. Brogan is required to pay $17,600 for the reasonably incurred attorney time of defendants' counsel relating to the Mr. Brogan's first and second privilege log, the motion for an order to show cause filed on January 19 and this motion. Mr. Brogan's first and second privilege logs lacked adequate grounds for the invocation of a privilege as to hundreds of documents for which Mr. Brogan claimed were privileged. There was no substantial justification for many of the entries on either the first or second privilege log. The deficiencies in the first and second privilege log led to defendants' incurrence of many hours of attorney time seeking to obtain the documents that were eventually produced on January 6, 2017, the filing of the motion for an order to show cause, and the filing of this motion. Defendants' request for more than $44,000 in monetary sanctions is excessive and appears to overstate the work needed to redress the discovery abuse associated with the first and second privilege logs. The court's best estimate is that defendants reasonably spent approximately 40 hours redressing the deficiencies in the first and second privilege. 40 multiplied by $440 is $17,600. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Laura A. Reding, CSR 711 =302/hk

Defendant Demeter Group Holdings L.P., Demeter Advisory Group, Llc, Jeff Menashe Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Feb-17-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jose-Manuel A. DeCastro, of DeCastro Law Group PC, Tel. 310.270.9877 ext 09817, for plaintiffs/cross-defendants, appearing via CourtCall;
Maiko Nakarai-Kanivas, of Littler Mendelson, P.C., Tel. 925.949.2385, for defendants;
Natalie A. Pierce, of Littler Mendelson, P.C., Tel. 415.288.6321, for defendants.

Ruling: A R G U E D. Defendants Demeter Group Holdings, L.P., Demeter Advisory Group, LLC and Jeff Menashe's motion for summary judgment is denied and their alternative motion for summary adjudication is denied as to plaintiff Chase Brogan's causes of action for breach of contract and fraud and granted as to Mr. Brogan's causes of action for violation of Labor Code 970 and accounting. As to the breach of contract cause of action, there is a triable issue whether the parties intended the January 27, 2015 e-mail to be a binding agreement. Mr. Brogan declares that he believed that the e-mail contained all of the material provisions and was never told otherwise. (Brogan Dec., pars. 11-12.) Mr. Mehashe on the other hand, claims that the agreement was not finalized. (Mehashe Dec., pars. 6-7). Defendants' reliance on Beck v. American Health Group International (1989) 211 Cal.App.3d 1555 (superseded by statute on other grounds) is misplaced. There, the court provides that "where it is part of the understanding between the parties that the terms of their contract are to be reduced to writing and signed by the parties, the assent to its terms must be evidenced in the manner agreed upon or it does not become a binding or completed contract." (Beck, 211 Cal.App.3d at 1562.) In Beck, "the writing itself was expressly denominated as an outline of a 'future agreement.'" (Harris v. Rudin, Richman & Appeal (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 299, 308.) In this case, the e-mail is prefaced with the following: "If we are in agreement, I will have employment counsel prepare letter for signature next week." That language does not clearly show an agreement to agree the promised written contract on several occasions. (Mr. Brogan's requests request for a formal written contract do not show as a matter of law that the parties did not have an agreement. There is also a triable issue whether the e-mail was too indefinite to enforce. The e-mail expressly states that the Ipsy fee split "will be treated separately" and the Ipsy transaction is unrelated to the bonus that Mr. Brogan currently seeks. The e-mail refers to a 90/10 bonus split and damages can be calculated. Defendants have not established the defense of unclean hands as a matter of law. Significantly Mr. Brogan told defendants of his relationship with Ipsy prior to becoming employed by Demeter. Defendants have not satisfied their summary adjudication burden on the fraud cause of action. Mr. Menashe's declaration does not clearly state that he intended to perform. Defendants point to how Mr. Menashe made a proposal to Mr. Brogan in June of 2015 that would have led to a large bonus, yet the relevant time frame is January 2015 when Mr. Menashe sent his email. Defendants' arguments that there was no misrepresentation or justifiable reliance because the e-mail was not a formal agreement have been rejected in connection with the breach of contract claim. Defendants are entitled to summary adjudication on the Labor Code 970 cause of action because Mr. Brogan admits that he maintained residences in both Beverly Hills and San Francisco from September 2014 through August 2016. Since Mr. Brogan already had a residence in San Francisco prior to the parties' relationship, there is no violation of section 970. Mr. Brogan does not oppose summary adjudication on his accounting cause of action. Prevailing party shall prepare form of order that complies with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Felicia Green; Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189, email: ckaren@sftc.org; Dept. 302.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Jan-23-2017 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Jose Manuel A. DeCastro of the law offices of DeCastro Law Group PC (310.270.9877 ext. 09817) appears via telephone as counsel for plaintiff/cross-defendant/moving party

Attorney, Natalie A. Pierce of the law offices of Littler Mendelson PC (415.288.6321) appears as counsel for defendant/opposing party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court partially reverses and partially confirms the tentative ruling.

PLAINTIFF/CROSS-DEFENDANT CHASE BROGAN's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication, as to all four causes of action alleged by Demeter Group Holdings, L.P. and Demeter Advisory Group, LLC in their first amended cross-complaint is in part GRANTED and in part DENIED. The motion is granted as to the first cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty. The motion is otherwise DENIED in its entirety. As to the first cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty, there are triable issues whether Mr. Brogan owed a fiduciary duty to Demeter and whether he breached that duty. GAB Bus. Servs., Inc. v. Lindsey & Newsom Claim Services, Inc. (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 409, 421 (overruled on other grounds in Reeves v. Hanlon (2004) 33 Cal.4th 1140) states that "an officer who participates in management of the corporation, exercising some discretionary authority, is a fiduciary of the corporation as a matter of law' Whether a particular officer participates in management is a question of fact." Demeter presented evidence that when discussing Mr. Brogan's proposed employment, it was understood that Mr. Brogan's role as "managing director" would include portfolio management, employee management, and consultation regarding personnel decisions and the firm's overall strategic direction. (Brogan Dec. Ex. 1; Mehashe Dec. par. 5; Menashe Depo. 26:15-24.) Demeter also presented evidence that Mr. Brogan: a) misstated the limited involvement that he would have with JeNu Biosciences, LLC while working for Demeter and actively worked for JeNu while serving as Demeter's employee (Menashe Depo. 32:15-33:3; Brogan Depo. 181:12-19; 227:10-228:15; 270:19-24; 291:5-8); b) secretly worked on the Ipsy account to Demeter's detriment while serving as Demeter's employee (Mehashe Dec. pars. 14-15; Brogan Dec. par. 24); c) provided advising services to Guthy Renker (Mr. Brogan's former employer) while working for Demeter (Brogan Depo. 466:7 - 470:6); and d) aided Guthy Renker in dealing with Demeter's competitors. (Mehashe Depo. 161:15-162:18.) As to the second cause of action for breach of the duty of loyalty, all employees owe a duty of loyalty to their employers (Stokes v. Dole Nut Co. (1995) 41 Cal.App.4th 285, 295-296) and the cited evidence shows that there is a triable issue whether Mr. Brogan breached that duty. As to the third cause of action for intentional interference with prospective advantage cause of action, the evidence showing that there is a triable issue that Mr. Brogan breached his duty of loyalty also suffices to show that there is a triable issue whether he committed wrongful conduct. There is also a triable issue whether Mr. Brogan interfered with Demeter's prospective advantage with respect to the Ipsy account and Guthy Renker. (Mehashe Depo. 161:15-162:18; 193:10-197:8.) All of the cited evidence also establishes that there is a triable issue as to the fourth cause of action for violation of the Unfair Competition Law whether Mr. Brogan engaged in unfair competition. The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.
